heuristics.rule: A world-class program with a bad advisor produces a worse outcome than a good program with an exceptional advisor
heuristics.condition: When choosing between a higher-ranked program with poor advisor fit and a lower-ranked program with strong advisor fit
heuristics.action: Prioritize advisor quality over program ranking
heuristics.because: The advisor controls funding continuity, research direction, mentorship quality, professional network access, reference letters, and graduation timeline — all of which outweigh brand name in determining actual career outcomes

heuristics.condition: When building your graduate school application portfolio
heuristics.action: Stay within the 8–12 range and never go below 8
heuristics.because: Fewer than 8 creates catastrophic risk in a bad admissions year; more than 12 yields diminishing returns given application fees, time cost, and marginal program quality

heuristics.rule: Confirm advisor interest before submitting to at least 5 programs on your list
heuristics.action: Contact potential advisors and secure demonstrated interest before submitting applications
heuristics.because: Cold applications with no prior faculty contact convert at significantly lower rates than applications where the advisor already knows your name and work

heuristics.rule: Ask former lab members 'Would you choose this advisor again knowing what you know now?'
heuristics.condition: When evaluating a potential PhD advisor
heuristics.action: Speak to 3–5 current and former students and ask this specific question
heuristics.because: Current students may be cautious; former students who have left the lab speak more freely and give the most honest signal of advisor quality

heuristics.rule: A funded PhD functions as a 5–6 year immigration runway
heuristics.condition: When an international student prioritizes US long-term residency but faces H-1B lottery uncertainty
heuristics.action: Factor the funded PhD's legal stay duration into the PhD vs. MS decision explicitly
heuristics.because: F-1 status during a funded PhD provides legal work authorization throughout, resets OPT post-graduation, and allows time to build a publication record qualifying for EB-1A or O-1 — both bypassing the H-1B lottery

heuristics.rule: Look at where the last 5 graduated students from the lab are now
heuristics.condition: When evaluating whether an advisor will actually advance your career
heuristics.action: Research the placement record of recent PhD graduates from the specific lab, not just the department
heuristics.because: Lab-level placement data reveals whether the advisor actively develops independent researchers or produces students who struggle to place — department averages can mask bad individual advisors

heuristics.rule: Check STEM Designated Degree Program List eligibility for your specific degree before applying
heuristics.condition: When OPT duration is a factor in your program selection
heuristics.action: Verify the specific degree (not just the field) is on the STEM OPT list
heuristics.because: STEM OPT extends work authorization from 12 to 36 months, but eligibility depends on the specific CIP code of your degree — not all programs in STEM fields qualify

decision_factors.factor: Advisor funding status and graduation record
decision_factors.why_it_matters: An advisor with expiring grants may lose ability to fund you; an advisor who hasn't graduated students in 3+ years signals mentorship or lab health problems
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask the advisor directly about current grant status; check NSF/NIH grant databases; ask current students about funding security; count funded graduates in the last 3 years

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Unfunded programs create financial pressure, immigration complications if finances are exhausted, and reduce negotiating leverage with advisors
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Verify the offer letter explicitly covers tuition and provides stipend; ask what happens to funding in years 4–6; clarify whether funding is guaranteed or contingent

decision_factors.factor: Program location relative to target job market
decision_factors.why_it_matters: During OPT, students must find employment while managing visa timing; programs in tech hubs have structurally more employers, alumni networks, and on-campus recruiting access
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Research whether target employers recruit on campus; check alumni LinkedIn profiles for post-graduation job locations; assess whether relocation during OPT is financially and logistically feasible

decision_factors.factor: Genuine interest in research as a practice
decision_factors.why_it_matters: PhD programs require 5–6 years of narrow, slow, often frustrating research — without intrinsic motivation, dropout risk is high (40–50% in many programs)
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Reflect honestly on prior research experience; ask whether you enjoyed the process of research, not just the outcomes; distinguish prestige motivation from intellectual motivation

decision_factors.factor: Funding type (RA vs. TA vs. Fellowship)
decision_factors.why_it_matters: RA ties your income to advisor's grant and constrains research direction; TA is more stable but costs 10–20 hours/week; fellowships give maximum independence
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask explicitly what type of funding your offer includes and whether it can change; ask whether you have flexibility to pursue your own research agenda vs. being required to work on the advisor's grants

decision_factors.factor: STEM OPT eligibility of specific degree
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Determines whether post-graduation work authorization is 12 or 36 months — a 2-year difference with major implications for H-1B lottery attempts and job search time
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check the DHS STEM Designated Degree Program List using the specific CIP code of the degree you will be awarded

decision_factors.factor: Track record of international student job placement
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Some programs have structural advantages or disadvantages in helping international students navigate the employer sponsorship process
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask current international students whether employers on campus offer sponsorship; check alumni profiles for post-graduation visa outcomes; ask the career office directly

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Longer time-to-degree extends total years on F-1 but also delays career entry and compresses the post-graduation OPT window relative to age and opportunity cost
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask the program directly; check with current students; look up published program statistics; ask specifically about funding continuity in years 4–6

decision_factors.factor: Advisor's behavior during the application process
decision_factors.why_it_matters: How an advisor treats you before you're their student predicts how they will treat you after — slow responses, lack of interest in your ideas, or dismissiveness are leading indicators
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Track response time to your emails; assess whether they engage substantively with your research ideas or give generic replies; notice whether they ask questions about your work

decision_factors.factor: PhD's advanced degree H-1B pool eligibility
decision_factors.why_it_matters: PhD graduates can enter the 20,000-slot advanced degree H-1B pool in addition to the general pool, improving lottery odds under wage-weighted systems
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Confirm the specific degree qualifies as an advanced degree for H-1B purposes; assess current H-1B policy and wage-weighting rules at time of application

decision_factors.why_it_matters: A lower-ranked program with a funded advisor whose work directly matches your interests may produce better career outcomes than a top-ranked program where you cannot find an advisor
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Map your specific research interests to individual faculty rather than department reputation; assess whether you can identify 2–3 faculty at each program who do work you'd genuinely want to contribute to

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Pursuing a PhD for prestige or immigration stability without genuine research interest
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: 40–50% dropout rate means a significant share of students leave after 2–3 years with no degree, no credential, and a major opportunity cost gap in their resume and visa timeline
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students who chose PhD primarily for immigration runway or institutional brand, without prior research experience to validate their tolerance for the work

hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Your research topic is partially or fully constrained by the advisor's active grant — your dissertation serves the advisor's funding priorities, not necessarily your intellectual agenda
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who have a specific research question or direction they want to pursue independently, who may find themselves locked into advisor-defined projects for years

hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Teaching 10–20 hours per week extends time-to-degree and reduces dissertation momentum — the 'stable' funding comes at the cost of slower research progress
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who underestimate the cognitive load of teaching preparation and grading, especially non-native English speakers who invest additional time in course communication

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Choosing a highly ranked program in a non-tech-hub location
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: During the OPT period, you face a 3–6 month structural disadvantage in job search compared to peers at hub-located programs — fewer employers recruit on campus, alumni networks are thinner in your target city, and relocation must happen while managing visa timing
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students whose target employers are concentrated in specific metro areas and who have limited financial buffer to fund an extended job search from a remote campus

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Relying on advisor continuity for funding and research support
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: If the advisor leaves the university, takes a leave, loses their grant, or retires, your funding and research support may collapse mid-degree with no automatic institutional backstop
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who did not ask explicitly about succession planning, co-advisor structures, or department policy for students whose advisors depart

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Pursuing an MS in the US as an immigration strategy
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: MS programs are typically 1–2 years, followed by 12–36 months OPT, after which you must win the H-1B lottery or leave — a funded PhD provides 5–6 additional years of legal stay before that clock starts
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who believe an MS degree resolves their long-term US immigration uncertainty, without accounting for H-1B lottery odds and the compressed timeline

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Applying to NSF GRFP as an international student
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: NSF GRFP is restricted to US citizens and permanent residents — international students who invest significant time in this application receive no benefit
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students who do not check fellowship eligibility before investing time in applications, often because the application process looks similar to eligible fellowships

failure_patterns.pattern: Pursuing a PhD primarily for prestige or immigration without genuine research interest
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: PhD programs carry cultural and social prestige signals; immigration anxiety causes students to optimize for visa runway rather than degree fit; students who haven't done research underestimate how different it is from coursework
failure_patterns.consequence: 40–50% dropout rate; students who leave after 2–3 years have no degree, a gap on their resume, disrupted visa status, and significant opportunity cost relative to entering industry earlier
warning_signs: Student cannot articulate a specific research question they want to pursue
Student's stated reason for PhD is 'more opportunities' or 'better visa situation' rather than specific intellectual or research goals

failure_patterns.pattern: Evaluating programs by ranking without screening individual advisors
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Rankings are visible and comparable; advisor quality requires effort to assess and is not publicly ranked; students conflate department reputation with individual mentor quality
failure_patterns.consequence: Student joins a high-ranked program with a poorly mentoring, under-funded, or exploitative advisor and experiences worse outcomes than they would have at a lower-ranked program with a strong advisor
warning_signs: Student lists programs only by US News ranking without identifying specific faculty
Student has not contacted or spoken to any faculty at their target programs before applying

failure_patterns.pattern: Applying to fewer than 8 programs to save time or application fees
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students underestimate year-to-year variability in admissions outcomes; overconfidence in their profile's competitiveness; cost sensitivity around application fees
failure_patterns.consequence: In a bad year — faculty on sabbatical, grant funding cuts, unusually competitive cohort — students with narrow application pools receive complete rejection and must reapply the following year, losing a full year of career progression
warning_signs: Student has applied to 5 or fewer programs
Student has not identified backup programs with confirmed advisor interest

failure_patterns.pattern: Submitting cold applications without prior advisor contact
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students assume the application process is anonymous and merit-based like undergraduate admissions; they don't realize faculty actively seek students whose names they already recognize
failure_patterns.consequence: Significantly lower admission conversion rate; student competes at a structural disadvantage against applicants whose names advisors already associate with their research agenda
warning_signs: Student has not emailed any faculty before submitting applications
Student's application list has no programs where they have confirmed faculty interest

failure_patterns.pattern: Ignoring the immigration implications of program and funding type selection
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students treat grad school choice as a purely academic or career decision; immigration consequences feel abstract and distant relative to immediate concerns about rankings and research
failure_patterns.consequence: Students reach graduation with a compressed OPT window, poor location for H-1B-sponsoring employers, or no path to extended legal stay — creating urgent visa crises during what should be a career transition period
warning_signs: Student cannot explain how their chosen program affects their OPT eligibility and duration
Student has not verified whether their specific degree is on the STEM Designated Degree Program List

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting funding offers without understanding what happens in years 4–6
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Funding offers are framed for entry years; students focus on the immediate offer rather than modeling long-term continuity; asking about late-stage funding feels awkward or pessimistic
failure_patterns.consequence: Students reach years 4–6 to find TA funding is competitive, RA grants have expired, and they must scramble for support while trying to finish dissertation — extending time-to-degree under financial stress
warning_signs: Student cannot explain what their funding source will be in years 4, 5, and 6
Offer letter specifies funding for only 1–2 years

diagnostic_questions.question: Is this program fully funded — tuition plus stipend — or will you be paying tuition?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has a financially viable path through the degree without accumulating debt or depleting savings, and whether they have correctly read their offer letter
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What happens to funding in years 4–6, and is it guaranteed or contingent on RA grant renewal?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Do you have verified financial resources to cover the full cost, and have you modeled what happens if your financial situation changes mid-program?

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you spoken to at least 2–3 current or former students in this specific advisor's lab?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has done real advisor due diligence or is relying on surface signals like publication count and program ranking
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Did you speak to anyone who has left the lab, and did you ask whether they would choose this advisor again?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Why not, and are you willing to delay your decision until you have done so?

diagnostic_questions.question: Do you genuinely want to do research, or are you pursuing a PhD for immigration stability, prestige, or funding?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has a motivational foundation strong enough to sustain 5–6 years of narrow, slow, often frustrating research work
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you already done research? What specifically did you enjoy about the process — not the outcomes, but the day-to-day work?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Have you considered whether a funded masters or direct industry entry would achieve your actual goals with less time and risk?

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your specific degree program on the STEM Designated Degree Program List?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student will qualify for 36-month STEM OPT or only 12-month standard OPT — a critical variable in post-graduation immigration runway
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you verified this using the specific CIP code of your exact degree, not just the field name?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: How does a 12-month OPT window change your job search and H-1B timeline planning?

diagnostic_questions.question: What happens to your visa status if you need to leave this program before completion?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has modeled downside scenarios and understands their immigration exposure if the PhD doesn't work out
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you consulted with an immigration advisor about your options in that scenario?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: This is a critical gap — a 40–50% PhD dropout rate means this scenario is not hypothetical for a large share of students

diagnostic_questions.question: Does this program have a track record of helping international students find jobs in your target market?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has verified the program's practical placement infrastructure for international students or is assuming it based on general reputation
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you verified this by speaking to international alumni rather than relying on program-published statistics?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Have you spoken to current international students about employer sponsorship availability and the on-campus recruiting landscape?

diagnostic_questions.question: What type of funding is included in your offer — RA, TA, or fellowship — and do you understand the constraints each imposes?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student understands how their funding type affects their research independence, time allocation, and risk exposure to advisor grant cycles
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If RA: have you confirmed whether you can pursue your own dissertation direction or must work on the advisor's active grant projects?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Do you know who to ask to get this clarified before accepting the offer?

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your program located in a city where your target employers actively recruit, or will you need to relocate during OPT?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has factored the structural job search advantage of tech-hub programs into their location decision
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you verified that employers in that city offer visa sponsorship at meaningful rates?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Have you modeled the financial and logistical cost of relocation during the OPT period while managing visa timing constraints?

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you identified at least one potential advisor at 5 or more programs on your list who has shown confirmed interest in your application?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student's application portfolio is built on strategic faculty contact or is a set of cold applications with low conversion probability
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: How did you confirm interest — email exchange, video call, or something else?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Which programs on your list have you contacted faculty at, and what was the response?

diagnostic_questions.question: What is the average time-to-degree in this program, and what does funding look like in years 4–6?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has stress-tested the financial and timeline assumptions behind their funding offer, or is only looking at the entry-year offer
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you spoken to students currently in years 4–6 about their funding experience, or are you relying on program-provided averages?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: This is a critical variable — a program with a 7-year average time-to-degree and only 4-year guaranteed funding creates a structural financial crisis for most students

industry_realities.common_belief: You are applying to a graduate program
industry_realities.actual_reality: In PhD admissions, you are primarily applying to an advisor — the program is the institutional container, but the advisor determines your funding, research direction, mentorship, network access, and career trajectory
industry_realities.implication: Spend more time researching and contacting individual faculty than researching departmental rankings or curriculum; a strong fit with a well-funded advisor at a mid-ranked program beats a cold application to a top-ranked department

industry_realities.common_belief: PhD admissions is a merit-based process where strong applications are evaluated anonymously
industry_realities.actual_reality: Faculty actively seek students whose names they already recognize — prior email contact, conference meetings, or research conversations give applicants a structural advantage over equally qualified cold applicants
industry_realities.implication: Contact potential advisors before submitting applications; introduce your research interests and ask substantive questions about their work; make your name visible before your application arrives

industry_realities.common_belief: A fully funded PhD offer means your funding is secure for the duration of the degree
industry_realities.actual_reality: RA funding is tied to the advisor's active grants, which can expire or be cut; TA funding is often competitive in later years; only multi-year fellowship funding is genuinely guaranteed — and even that has conditions
industry_realities.implication: Ask explicitly about funding continuity in years 4–6 before accepting any offer; understand which specific mechanism funds you and what happens if that mechanism ends

industry_realities.common_belief: Choosing a top-ranked program maximizes career outcomes
industry_realities.actual_reality: A world-class program with a bad advisor produces worse career outcomes than a good program with an exceptional advisor — advisor quality, funding continuity, and research fit are stronger predictors of PhD outcome than department ranking
industry_realities.implication: Evaluate programs at the level of individual faculty labs, not aggregate rankings; a strong advisor at a program ranked 15–30 is likely a better choice than a weak or unavailable advisor at a top-5 program

industry_realities.common_belief: The NSF Graduate Research Fellowship is a viable funding option for international students to pursue
industry_realities.actual_reality: NSF GRFP is restricted to US citizens and permanent residents — international students are categorically ineligible
industry_realities.implication: International students should not invest application time in NSF GRFP; check eligibility for every fellowship before investing effort, as eligibility varies significantly across external fellowship programs

industry_realities.common_belief: An MS degree resolves long-term US immigration uncertainty
industry_realities.actual_reality: An MS provides 12–36 months of OPT, after which you must win the H-1B lottery or leave; a funded PhD provides 5–6 years of legal stay, resets OPT, and builds credentials that may qualify for lottery-exempt visas
industry_realities.implication: International students who care about long-term US immigration stability should explicitly model the PhD vs. MS decision through an immigration lens, not just a career or tuition lens

industry_realities.common_belief: PhD dropout is rare and mainly affects students who were clearly not ready
industry_realities.actual_reality: PhD dropout rates are 40–50% in many programs — this is not a fringe outcome; it represents a large share of admitted students who often leave 2–3 years in with no degree and significant opportunity cost
industry_realities.implication: The risk of leaving a PhD without a degree is not a tail risk — it is a base rate outcome for a near-majority of students; this should be explicitly weighted in the PhD vs. MS decision, especially for students without prior research experience

industry_realities.common_belief: University-issued fellowship funding is multi-year and stable
industry_realities.actual_reality: University fellowships given to top incoming students are often 1-year awards, not multi-year guarantees; they are frequently used as recruiting incentives without long-term funding commitments
industry_realities.implication: Distinguish explicitly between a 1-year university fellowship and a multi-year external fellowship; ask what your funding source will be in years 2–6 before treating a fellowship offer as evidence of long-term financial security

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Unit of analysis in program evaluation
junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Evaluates graduate programs by US News ranking and general departmental reputation
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Evaluates graduate programs by individual advisor quality, lab funding status, recent graduate placement, and confirmed advisor interest
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The advisor — not the department — determines research direction, funding security, mentorship quality, and career network; optimizing for ranking without advisor-level analysis produces systematically worse outcomes

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Pursues PhD because it 'sounds prestigious,' provides immigration runway, or because 'more education means more opportunities'
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Pursues PhD only after verifying genuine interest in research as a practice through prior research experience, and can articulate a specific intellectual problem they want to work on
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: PhD programs have 40–50% dropout rates; students without intrinsic research motivation disproportionately contribute to that rate, exiting after 2–3 years with no degree and significant cost

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: How immigration implications are factored into grad school decisions
junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats grad school choice as purely academic or career-focused; assumes immigration 'will work out' or is a problem for after graduation
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Explicitly models how program location, degree type, STEM OPT eligibility, funding duration, and credential type interact with post-graduation visa pathways before choosing a program
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Students who don't model immigration implications face visa crises during career transitions — compressed OPT windows, poor employer sponsorship access, or lack of H-1B lottery alternatives

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Evaluates advisors by publication count, h-index, and lab website — all public, surface-level signals
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Speaks to 3–5 current and former students, specifically asks whether they would choose the advisor again, checks where the last 5 graduates are now, and tracks advisor behavior during the application process as a leading indicator
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Surface signals don't reveal mentorship quality, lab culture, funding stability, or whether the advisor develops independent researchers — the variables that actually determine degree experience and career outcomes

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Sees 'fully funded' in an offer letter and treats financial security as confirmed; doesn't distinguish funding types or ask about year 4–6 continuity
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Asks explicitly what type of funding is offered (RA/TA/fellowship), what grant or mechanism funds it, what happens if that mechanism ends, and what funding looks like in years 4–6
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: RA funding tied to expiring grants can collapse mid-degree; TA positions become competitive in later years; students who don't model this face financial crises during dissertation completion

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Applies to a small number of programs to save time and fees, weighted toward reach schools based on ranking
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Constructs a balanced 8–12 program portfolio with 2–3 reach, 4–5 core, and 2–3 safety programs, with confirmed advisor interest in at least 5 programs before submitting
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Year-to-year admissions variability means even strong applicants face complete rejection with narrow portfolios; confirmed advisor interest dramatically increases conversion rates and reduces portfolio risk

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Applies for well-known fellowships like NSF GRFP without checking eligibility for international students
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Checks eligibility requirements for every fellowship before investing application effort, recognizing that major US fellowships vary significantly in international student eligibility
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: NSF GRFP is restricted to US citizens and permanent residents — international students who invest time in ineligible applications lose opportunity to identify and apply for fellowships they actually qualify for
